title: Symmetrizing Bregman Divergence on the Cone of Positive Definite Matrices: Which Mean to Use and Why
abstract: This work uncovers variational principles behind symmetrizing the Bregman divergences induced by generic mirror maps over the cone of positive definite matrices. We show that computing the canonical means for this symmetrization can be posed as minimizing the desired symmetrized divergences over a set of mean functionals defined axiomatically to satisfy certain properties. For the forward symmetrization, we prove that the arithmetic mean over the primal space is canonical for any mirror map over the positive definite cone. For the reverse symmetrization, we show that the canonical mean is the arithmetic mean over the dual space, pulled back to the primal space. Applying this result to three common mirror maps used in practice, we show that the canonical means for reverse symmetrization, in those cases, turn out to be the arithmetic, log-Euclidean and harmonic means. Our results improve understanding of existing symmetrization practices in the literature, and can be seen as a navigational chart to help decide which mean to use when.

sections.heading: IIntroduction
sections.text:
The Bregman divergence [ 1 ] , [ 2 , Ch. 2.1] , [ 3 , 4 , 5 ] and its symmetrized variants [ 6 , 7 ] have found widespread usage across control, optimization, and machine learning [ 8 , 9 , 10 , 11 , 12 , 13 , 14 ] . Intuitively, such divergences are squared distance-like functionals [ 15 , p. 10] over appropriate domains. These functionals are not symmetric in general, but can be made so by a symmetrization procedure. This work focuses on the choice of mean used for the symmetrization of a class of Bregman divergence.

To distill the problem, let us first introduce the background ideas and notations.

Geometry of Positive Definite Matrices. Let 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} denote the open convex cone of n × n n\times n positive definite matrices, i.e., 𝕊 + + n := { X ∈ ℝ n × n ∣ X ⊤ = X , \displaystyle\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++}:=\{X\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\mid X^{\top}=X, v ⊤ ​ X ​ v > 0 \displaystyle\;v^{\top}Xv>0 ∀ nonzero vector v ∈ ℝ n } . \displaystyle\forall\>\text{nonzero vector}\;v\in\mathbb{R}^{n}\}. Likewise, 𝕊 − − n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{--} denotes the convex cone of n × n n\times n negative definite matrices.

Any X ∈ 𝕊 + + n X\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} admits polar decomposition [ 16 , Thm. 7.3.1] X = Y ​ Y ⊤ = ( U ​ Z ) ​ ( U ​ Z ) ⊤ = U ​ Z 2 ​ U ⊤ , \displaystyle X=YY^{\top}=(UZ)(UZ)^{\top}=UZ^{2}U^{\top}, (1) where Y ∈ GL ​ ( n ) Y\in{\mathrm{GL}}(n) (the set of n × n n\times n invertible matrices with real entries), U ∈ O ​ ( n ) U\in{\mathrm{O}}(n) (the set of n × n n\times n orthogonal matrices), and Z ∈ 𝕊 + + n Z\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} . The decomposition ( 1 ) highlights the quotient geometry 𝕊 + + n ≅ GL ​ ( n ) / O ​ ( n ) ≅ ( O ​ ( n ) × 𝕊 + + n ) / O ​ ( n ) , \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++}\cong{\mathrm{GL}}(n)/{\mathrm{O}}(n)\cong\left({\mathrm{O}}(n)\times\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++}\right)/{\mathrm{O}}(n), which implies a GL ​ ( n ) {\mathrm{GL}}(n) -invariant Riemannian metric on 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} , referred to as the natural metric [ 17 , 18 ] . Recall that at any A ∈ 𝕊 + + n A\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} , the tangent space T A ​ 𝕊 + + n ⊆ 𝕊 n {\mathrm{T}}_{A}\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++}\subseteq\mathbb{S}^{n} (the set of n × n n\times n symmetric matrices), and the GL ​ ( n ) {\mathrm{GL}}(n) -invariant metric at the tangent space at identity is equipped with the Frobenius inner product, i.e., g I ​ ( X , Y ) := ⟨ X , Y ⟩ = tr ⁡ ( X ​ Y ) g_{I}(X,Y):=\langle X,Y\rangle=\tr(XY) . So the GL ​ ( n ) {\mathrm{GL}}(n) -invariant metric at arbitrary A ∈ 𝕊 + + n A\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} must be given by [ 19 , 18 , 20 ] g A ​ ( X , Y ) := ⟨ X , ( ℒ A ∘ ℛ A ) − 1 ​ Y ⟩ = trace ​ ( A − 1 ​ X ​ A − 1 ​ Y ) , \displaystyle g_{A}(X,Y):=\langle X,\left(\mathcal{L}_{A}\circ\mathcal{R}_{A}\right)^{-1}Y\rangle={\mathrm{trace}}\!\left(\!A^{-1}XA^{-1}Y\right), (2) where ℒ A ​ X := A ​ X \mathcal{L}_{A}X:=AX (left multiplication by A A ), and ℛ A ​ X := X ​ A \mathcal{R}_{A}X:=XA (right multiplication by A A ).

Endowed with ( 2 ), we view 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} as a Riemannian manifold of nonpositive curvature [ 21 , p. 226-227] . In this manifold, any 𝒞 1 \mathcal{C}^{1} curve γ : [ 0 , 1 ] → 𝕊 + + n \gamma:[0,1]\to\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} has length ∫ 0 1 ‖ γ − 1 2 ​ ( t ) ​ γ ′ ​ ( t ) ​ γ − 1 2 ​ ( t ) ‖ F ​ d t \int_{0}^{1}\|\gamma^{-\frac{1}{2}}(t)\gamma^{\prime}(t)\gamma^{-\frac{1}{2}}(t)\|_{\mathrm{F}}\>\differential t where ∥ ⋅ ∥ F \|\cdot\|_{\mathrm{F}} denotes the Frobenius norm, and ′ denotes the derivative. For fixed A , B ∈ 𝕊 + + n A,B\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} , the minimal Riemannian geodesic curve γ opt ​ ( t ) := \displaystyle\gamma^{\mathrm{opt}}(t):= arg ​ inf γ ​ ( ⋅ ) ∈ 𝒞 1 ​ ( [ 0 , 1 ] ; 𝕊 + + n ) ​ ∫ 0 1 ‖ γ − 1 2 ​ ( t ) ​ γ ′ ​ ( t ) ​ γ − 1 2 ​ ( t ) ‖ F ​ d t , \displaystyle\underset{\gamma(\cdot)\in\mathcal{C}^{1}\left([0,1];\>\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++}\right)}{\arg\inf}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}\|\gamma^{-\frac{1}{2}}(t)\gamma^{\prime}(t)\gamma^{-\frac{1}{2}}(t)\|_{\mathrm{F}}\>\differential t, subject ​ to γ ​ ( 0 ) = A , γ ​ ( 1 ) = B . \displaystyle\quad\mathrm{subject\,to}\quad\gamma(0)=A,\quad\gamma(1)=B. (3) In particular [ 22 , 23 ] , γ opt ​ ( t ) = A 1 2 ​ ( A − 1 2 ​ B ​ A − 1 2 ) t ​ A 1 2 ∀ t ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] . \displaystyle\gamma^{\mathrm{opt}}(t)=A^{\frac{1}{2}}\left(A^{-\frac{1}{2}}BA^{-\frac{1}{2}}\right)^{t}A^{\frac{1}{2}}\quad\forall t\in[0,1]. (4) Bregman divergence on 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} . The Bregman divergence on 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} is induced by a mapping ψ : 𝕊 + + n ↦ ℝ \psi:\mathbb{S}_{++}^{n}\mapsto\mathbb{R} , where ψ \psi is a strictly convex 𝒞 2 \mathcal{C}^{2} function of Legendre type [ 24 , p. 258] . In the mirror descent literature [ 25 , Sec. 4.1] , [ 26 , 12 ] , ψ \psi is referred to as the mirror map . The formal definition for Bregman divergence is as follows.

Geometrically, D ψ D_{\psi} quantifies the error at X ∈ 𝕊 + + n X\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} due to first-order Taylor approximation of ψ \psi at Y ∈ 𝕊 + + n Y\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} .

It is straightforward to see that D ψ ≥ 0 D_{\psi}\geq 0 ∀ X , Y ∈ 𝕊 + + n \forall X,Y\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} , and D ψ = 0 D_{\psi}=0 iff X = Y ∈ 𝕊 + + n X=Y\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} . Yet D ψ D_{\psi} is not a metric in general 1 1 1 An exception is the case ψ ​ ( X ) = ‖ X ‖ F 2 \psi(X)=\|X\|_{\mathrm{F}}^{2} (squared Frobenius norm) which yields D ψ ​ ( X , Y ) = ‖ X − Y ‖ F 2 D_{\psi}(X,Y)=\|X-Y\|_{\mathrm{F}}^{2} , see first row of Table I . due to lack of symmetry (i.e., D ψ ​ ( X , Y ) ≠ D ψ ​ ( Y , X ) D_{\psi}(X,Y)\neq D_{\psi}(Y,X) ) and failure to satisfy triangle inequality. Interestingly, D ψ D_{\psi} is a convex function w.r.t. its first argument.

Table I lists well-known [ 4 , 5 ] examples of ψ , D ψ \psi,D_{\psi} on 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} . See also [ 9 , Table 15.1] . In particular, the mirror map ψ \psi in the first row of Table I is the squared Frobenius norm, which induces the squared Frobenius norm of the difference as D ψ D_{\psi} .

The mirror map ψ \psi in the second row of Table I is the negative von Neumann entropy [ 27 , Ch. 13] , which induces Umegaki’s quantum relative entropy [ 28 , 29 ] as D ψ D_{\psi} .

The mirror map ψ \psi in the third row of Table I is the Burg’s entropy 2 2 2 Burg’s entropy is the natural self-concordant barrier for 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} , and is used in interior point algorithms [ 30 , Ch. 5.4] . [ 31 ] , which induces (twice of) the classical relative entropy or the Kullback-Leibler divergence between centered Gaussian distributions as D ψ D_{\psi} .

Symmetrization of D ψ D_{\psi} on 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} . One way to fix the lack of symmetry in ( 5 ) is to consider the Jeffrey’s symmetrization [ 6 ] : 1 2 ​ { D ψ ​ ( X ∥ Y ) + D ψ ​ ( Y ∥ X ) } \frac{1}{2}\{D_{\psi}(X\parallel Y)+D_{\psi}(Y\parallel X)\} . In this work, we focus on a different class of symmetrized Bregman divergence , defined as D ψ symm → ​ ( X , Y ; M ) \displaystyle D_{\psi}^{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{symm}}}(X,Y;M) := 1 2 ​ { D ψ ​ ( X ∥ M ​ ( X , Y ) ) + D ψ ​ ( Y ∥ M ​ ( X , Y ) ) } , \displaystyle:=\dfrac{1}{2}\bigg\{D_{\psi}\left(X\parallel M(X,Y)\right)+D_{\psi}\left(Y\parallel M(X,Y)\right)\bigg\}, (6) or as D ψ symm ← ​ ( X , Y ; M ) \displaystyle D_{\psi}^{\overleftarrow{\mathrm{symm}}}(X,Y;M) := 1 2 ​ { D ψ ​ ( M ​ ( X , Y ) ∥ X ) + D ψ ​ ( M ​ ( X , Y ) ∥ Y ) } , \displaystyle:=\dfrac{1}{2}\bigg\{D_{\psi}\left(M(X,Y)\parallel X\right)+D_{\psi}\left(M(X,Y)\parallel Y\right)\bigg\}, (7) where M ​ ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) M(\cdot,\cdot) is a mean on 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} given axiomatically in Definition 2 below. Notice that the “forward” symmetrization ( 6 ), and the “reverse” symmetrization ( 7 ) yield different functionals in general, due to the lack of symmetry of D ψ D_{\psi} .

In the special case n = 1 n=1 , 𝕊 + + n ≡ ℝ > 0 \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++}\equiv\mathbb{R}_{>0} . In that case, using lowercase for scalars, familiar examples of M M are arithmetic mean, geometric mean, harmonic mean, logarithmic mean, given respectively as a + b 2 , a ​ b , ( a − 1 + b − 1 2 ) − 1 , ∫ 0 1 a t ​ b 1 − t ​ d t . \frac{a+b}{2},\quad\sqrt{ab},\quad\left(\frac{a^{-1}+b^{-1}}{2}\right)^{\!\!-1}\!\!\!\!,\quad\int_{0}^{1}a^{t}b^{1-t}\differential t. Per Definition 2 , these examples generalize on 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} , respectively, as A + B 2 , A 1 2 ​ ( A − 1 2 ​ B ​ A − 1 2 ) 1 2 ​ A 1 2 , ( A − 1 + B − 1 2 ) − 1 , ∫ 0 1 A t ​ B 1 − t ​ d t . \displaystyle\dfrac{A+B}{2},\,A^{\frac{1}{2}}\!\!\left(\!A^{-\frac{1}{2}}BA^{-\frac{1}{2}}\!\right)^{\!\!\frac{1}{2}}\!\!A^{\frac{1}{2}},\,\left(\!\!\dfrac{A^{-1}+B^{-1}}{2}\!\right)^{\!\!\!-1}\!\!\!\!,\int_{0}^{1}\!\!\!A^{t}B^{1-t}\differential t.

Motivated by Remark 4 , we define the following two sets of means.

To exemplify Definition 3 , we note that the arithmetic, geometric, harmonic and logarithmic means over 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} are in ℳ \mathcal{M} . The log-Euclidean mean ( 9 ) is in ℳ ^ \widehat{\mathcal{M}} but not in ℳ \mathcal{M} .

Motivation. Now that we have the definition and examples for the generalized mean M M in place, let us return to ( 6 ). We think of D ψ symm → D_{\psi}^{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{symm}}} in ( 6 ) to be parameterized by a choice of generalized mean M M . If we choose M M to be the arithmetic mean in ( 6 ), then D ψ symm → D_{\psi}^{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{symm}}} reduces to the well-known Stein or Jensen-Shannon symmetrization [ 6 ] ψ ​ ( X ) + ψ ​ ( Y ) 2 − ψ ​ ( X + Y 2 ) , \displaystyle\dfrac{\psi(X)+\psi(Y)}{2}-\psi\left(\dfrac{X+Y}{2}\right), (11) which follows from ( 5 ). In particular, [ 32 ] showed that specializing ( 11 ) with ψ ​ ( X ) = − log ⁡ det ​ X \psi(X)=-\log{\mathrm{det}}X yields a squared distance functional named therein as the S S -divergence 4 4 4 The S S -divergence can be interpreted as the Bhattacharya distance [ 33 ] between two centered Gaussian probability distributions, but is not a metric. See also [ 34 ] . . In other words, the square root of ( 11 ) then is a metric on 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} .

These observations raise natural questions: • is there something special about the choice of M M as arithmetic mean in ( 6 ) as the literature on specific cases seem to suggest? • what should be the canonical choice of M M in ( 7 )? • should the choice of canonical M M be affected by the specificity of ψ \psi ? The mathematical developments in this work are motivated by these questions.

Contributions and related works. The purpose of this work is to clarify canonical choices of M M for ( 6 ) and ( 7 ). Specifically, we formulate variational principles that guide the canonical choices of the mean M M independent of ψ \psi .

To the best of our knowledge, the questions we posed above regarding the symmetrization ( 6 ) and ( 7 ) over 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} , have not been investigated before. The related work in [ 6 ] is in a different spirit: it focuses on a statistical viewpoint and considers a specific symmetrization and specific ψ \psi resulting in the Kullback-Leibler divergence.

By answering the aforesaid questions, we improve the understanding of Bregman symmetrization, specifically by showing that the choice of M M is not ad hoc, but is principled by variational reasoning.

Organization. The remaining of this work is structured as follows. Sec. II derives the main results, viz. the optimal choice of canonical means (Theorem 1 and Theorem 3 ), and the corresponding canonical forward and reverse symmetrized Bregman divergences (Corollary 2 and Corollary 4 ). Specifically, Theorem 1 shows that for forward symmetrization, the arithmetic mean is canonical irrespective of the choice of mirror map ψ \psi . Theorem 3 establishes that for reverse symmetrization, the canonical mean is obtained by first computing the arithmetic mean in dual space, and then pulling it back to the primal space. Sec. III details the computational specifics for the three examples in Table I , thereby illustrating the results from Sec. II for those three commonly used mirror maps on 𝕊 + + n \mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} . Sec. IV concludes the work.

sections.heading: IIMain Results
sections.text: We seek to choose the “best” M M for fixed X , Y ∈ 𝕊 + + n X,Y\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} for the symmetrized Bregman divergence functionals ( 6 ) or ( 7 ). Since these functionals are, by definition, non-negative and symmetric, it is natural to define the canonical mean M M as the least conservative choice, i.e., the M M which minimize ( 6 ) or ( 7 ). Thus motivated, we define M → canonical := arg ⁡ min M ∈ ℳ ​ D ψ symm → ​ ( X , Y ; M ) , \displaystyle\overrightarrow{M}_{\mathrm{canonical}}:=\underset{M\in\mathcal{M}}{\arg\min}\;D_{\psi}^{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{symm}}}(X,Y;M), (12a) M ← canonical := arg ⁡ min M ∈ ℳ ^ ​ D ψ symm ← ​ ( X , Y ; M ) . \displaystyle\overleftarrow{M}_{\mathrm{canonical}}:=\underset{M\in\widehat{\mathcal{M}}}{\arg\min}\;D_{\psi}^{\overleftarrow{\mathrm{symm}}}(X,Y;M). (12b) The reason for using ℳ ^ \widehat{\mathcal{M}} instead of ℳ \mathcal{M} in ( 12b ) will be explained in Sec. II-B (proof of Theorem 3 and Remark 6 ). At this point, it is not a priori clear if such minimizers exist or unique or even if they do, whether they depend on the choice of ψ \psi in addition to the fixed data X , Y ∈ 𝕊 + + n X,Y\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} . Last but not the least, it is unclear how to perform the minimization since it is not apparent how to parameterize ℳ \mathcal{M} or ℳ ^ \widehat{\mathcal{M}} .

sections.heading: II-A​​Arithmetic Mean is Canonical for Forward Symmetrization
sections.text:
Our first result concerns with ( 12a ), i.e., the choice of canonical mean in forward Bregman symmetrization ( 6 ).

The next Corollary follows by substituting ( 13 ) back in ( 6 ), and then using ( 5 ).

sections.heading: II-BArithmetic Mean in the Dual Space is Canonical for Reverse Symmetrization
sections.text:
Our next result concerns with ( 12b ), i.e., the choice of canonical mean in reverse Bregman symmetrization ( 7 ).

Unlike Theorem 1 , the next result requires further assumptions on ψ \psi .

The next Corollary follows by substituting ( 16 ) back in ( 7 ), and then using ( 5 ).

It is insightful to compare ( 20 ) with its forward analogue ( 11 ), namely the Jensen-Shannon symmetrization. They have similar forms: the subtraction of the average by a primal/dual evaluation of the primal/dual average.

sections.heading: IIIExamples
sections.text: We next give some details for the three examples mentioned in Table I since these are encountered frequently in applications. For the readers’ convenience, a summary of the following is presented in Table II .

sections.heading: III-AThe Caseψ​(X)=‖X‖F2\psi(X)=\|X\|_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}
sections.text:
Here, the gradient ∇ ψ ​ ( X ) = 2 ​ X ∈ 𝕊 + + n ⊂ 𝕊 n \nabla\psi(X)=2X\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++}\subset\mathbb{S}^{n} , and the Hessian H ​ ψ ​ ( X ) = 2 ​ ( I n ⊗ I n ) ∈ 𝕊 + + n {\mathrm{H}}\psi(X)=2\left(I_{n}\otimes I_{n}\right)\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} where I n I_{n} denotes the n × n n\times n identity matrix, and ⊗ \otimes denotes the Kronecker product.

In this case, ψ ∗ ​ ( Y ) = 1 4 ​ trace ​ ( Y 2 ) \psi^{*}(Y)=\frac{1}{4}{\mathrm{trace}}(Y^{2}) where Y ∈ 𝕊 + + n Y\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} , and ∇ ψ ∗ ​ ( Y ) = 1 2 ​ Y \nabla\psi^{*}(Y)=\frac{1}{2}Y , which from ( 13 ) and ( 16 ) yields M → canonical = M ← canonical = X + Y 2 . \overrightarrow{M}_{\mathrm{canonical}}=\overleftarrow{M}_{\mathrm{canonical}}=\frac{X+Y}{2}. Since the arithmetic mean is GL ​ ( n ) {\mathrm{GL}}(n) invariant, the optimizer M ← canonical \overleftarrow{M}_{\mathrm{canonical}} for this ψ \psi is, in fact, in ℳ \mathcal{M} .

From ( 11 ) and ( 20 ), the symmetrized Bregman divergences D ψ symm → = D ψ symm ← = 1 4 ​ ‖ X − Y ‖ F 2 . D_{\psi}^{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{symm}}}=D_{\psi}^{\overleftarrow{\mathrm{symm}}}=\frac{1}{4}\|X-Y\|_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}.

sections.heading: III-BThe Caseψ​(X)=trace​(X​log⁡X−X)\psi(X)={\mathrm{trace}}(X\log X-X)
sections.text:
We find the gradient ∇ ψ ​ ( X ) = log ⁡ X ∈ 𝕊 n \nabla\psi(X)=\log X\in\mathbb{S}^{n} where log \log denotes the principal logarithm, and the Hessian H ​ ψ ​ ( X ) = ∫ 0 ∞ ( ( t ​ I n + X ) − 1 ⊗ ( t ​ I n + X ) − 1 ) ​ d t ∈ 𝕊 + + n {\mathrm{H}}\psi(X)=\int_{0}^{\infty}\left((tI_{n}+X)^{-1}\otimes(tI_{n}+X)^{-1}\right)\differential t\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} , see e.g., [ 37 , Lemma 2(ii)] .

Direct computation gives ψ ∗ ​ ( Y ) = trace ​ ( exp ⁡ Y ) \psi^{*}(Y)={\mathrm{trace}}(\exp Y) , and ∇ ψ ∗ ​ ( Y ) = exp ⁡ Y \nabla\psi^{*}(Y)=\exp Y . From ( 13 ) and ( 16 ), M → canonical = X + Y 2 , M ← canonical = exp ⁡ ( log ⁡ X + log ⁡ Y 2 ) , \overrightarrow{M}_{\mathrm{canonical}}=\frac{X+Y}{2},\;\overleftarrow{M}_{\mathrm{canonical}}=\exp\left(\frac{\log X+\log Y}{2}\right), namely the arithmetic and log-Euclidean means, respectively. Since the log-Euclidean mean is O ​ ( n ) {\mathrm{O}}(n) invariant, the optimizer M ← canonical \overleftarrow{M}_{\mathrm{canonical}} is in ℳ ^ \widehat{\mathcal{M}} but not in ℳ \mathcal{M} .

From ( 11 ), we get the forward symmetrized Bregman divergence D ψ symm → \displaystyle D_{\psi}^{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{symm}}} = 1 2 ​ trace ​ ( X ​ log ⁡ X + Y ​ log ⁡ Y ) \displaystyle=\frac{1}{2}{\mathrm{trace}}(X\log X+Y\log Y) − trace ​ ( X + Y 2 ​ log ⁡ X + Y 2 ) , \displaystyle\qquad\qquad-{\mathrm{trace}}\left(\frac{X+Y}{2}\log\frac{X+Y}{2}\right), and from ( 20 ), the reverse symmetrized Bregman divergence D ψ symm ← = \displaystyle D_{\psi}^{\overleftarrow{\mathrm{symm}}}= trace ​ ( X ​ log ⁡ X + Y ​ log ⁡ Y 2 ) − trace ​ ( X + Y 2 ) \displaystyle{\mathrm{trace}}\left(\dfrac{X\log X+Y\log Y}{2}\right)-{\mathrm{trace}}\left(\dfrac{X+Y}{2}\right) − trace ​ ( exp ⁡ ( log ⁡ X + log ⁡ Y 2 ) ) . \displaystyle\qquad\qquad-{\mathrm{trace}}\left(\exp\left(\frac{\log X+\log Y}{2}\right)\right).

sections.heading: III-CThe Caseψ​(X)=−log⁡det​X\psi(X)=-\log{\mathrm{det}}X
sections.text:
In this case, we compute the gradient ∇ ψ = − X − 1 ∈ 𝕊 − − n ⊂ 𝕊 n \nabla\psi=-X^{-1}\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{--}\subset\mathbb{S}^{n} , and the Hessian H ​ ψ ​ ( X ) = ( X − 1 ⊗ X − 1 ) ∈ 𝕊 + + n {\mathrm{H}}\psi(X)=\left(X^{-1}\otimes X^{-1}\right)\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{++} .

We next find ψ ∗ ​ ( Y ) = − n − log ⁡ det ​ ( − Y ) \psi^{*}(Y)=-n-\log{\mathrm{det}}(-Y) where Y ∈ 𝕊 − − n Y\in\mathbb{S}^{n}_{--} , and ∇ ψ ∗ ​ ( Y ) = − Y − 1 \nabla\psi^{*}(Y)=-Y^{-1} . Then, ( 13 ) and ( 16 ) give M → canonical = X + Y 2 , M ← canonical = ( X − 1 + Y − 1 2 ) − 1 , \overrightarrow{M}_{\mathrm{canonical}}=\frac{X+Y}{2},\;\overleftarrow{M}_{\mathrm{canonical}}=\left(\frac{X^{-1}+Y^{-1}}{2}\right)^{\!\!-1}, namely the arithmetic and harmonic means, respectively. Since the harmonic mean is GL ​ ( n ) {\mathrm{GL}}(n) invariant, the optimizer M ← canonical \overleftarrow{M}_{\mathrm{canonical}} for this ψ \psi is, in fact, in ℳ \mathcal{M} .

From ( 11 ), we obtain the forward symmetrized Bregman divergence D ψ symm → \displaystyle D_{\psi}^{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{symm}}} = log ⁡ det ​ ( X + Y 2 ) − 1 2 ​ log ⁡ det ​ ( X ​ Y ) , \displaystyle=\log{\mathrm{det}}\left(\frac{X+Y}{2}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\log{\mathrm{det}}(XY), which is the S S -divergence [ 32 ] . Using ( 20 ), the reverse symmetrized Bregman divergence in this case becomes D ψ symm ← = log ⁡ det ​ ( X − 1 + Y − 1 2 ) + 1 2 ​ log ⁡ det ​ ( X − 1 ​ Y − 1 ) + n . \displaystyle D_{\psi}^{\overleftarrow{\mathrm{symm}}}=\!\log{\mathrm{det}}\!\!\left(\!\!\frac{X^{-1}+Y^{-1}}{2}\!\right)\!+\frac{1}{2}\log{\mathrm{det}}(X^{-1}Y^{-1})+n.

sections.heading: IVConclusion
sections.text:
Bregman divergences and their symmetrizations find broad usage across control, optimization and learning algorithms. For specific mirror maps such as the Burg entropy and the negative von Neumann entropy, existing literature commonly use the arithmetic mean for symmetrizing the Bregman divergence on the cone positive definite matrices. Driven by the intuition that there might be variational principles lurking behind the scene, we investigated if there could be a notion of canonical mean for this symmetrization procedure for generic mirror maps . We proposed variational formulations for the canonical means. We showed that for forward symmetrization, the arithmetic mean is canonical irrespective of the choice of the mirror map. For reverse symmetrization, we proved that the canonical mean is obtained by first computing the arithmetic mean in dual space, and then pulling it back to the primal space. We derived the corresponding symmetrized Bregman divergences in terms of their mirror maps. These results were exemplified via three mirror maps commonly used for the cone of positive definite matrices.

While we focused on the forward and reverse symmetrizations ( 6 ) and ( 7 ), as they are most common in literature, it is possible to extend our analyses for other symmetrizations, such as the geometric and harmonic a.k.a. resistor symmetrizations [ 6 ] , and regularized variants [ 38 , Sec. 3] . These will be pursued in future work.
